title: Migration Between Vector Databases
concept: The practical steps and pitfalls of moving an existing embedding index from one store to another.

exercises.prompt: When selecting a vector database (recap from last lesson), what should you focus on to what should you pay), what is a key feature comparison lesson), what should lookback from our prior lesson), what should be your primary focus?

options.text: Evaluating features like hybrid search support and ecosystem integration.
Picking the database with the prettiest logo.
Assuming all vector databases have the exact same metadata filtering syntax.

exercises.explain: In the prior lesson, we covered criteria like filtering support, hybrid search capabilities, scaling models, and ecosystem integration to choose the right database.

cards.title: The Migration Challenge
cards.body: Choosing the perfect vector database is great, but what happens when your application outgrows it? Moving millions of high-dimensional vectors to a new home requires careful planning to avoid data loss and massive API bills.

cards.title: Vector Database Migration
cards.body: Vector database migration is the process of moving vector embeddings, their unique IDs, and associated metadata from one storage provider to another without losing data structure or indexing details.

cards.title: The Library Relocation
cards.body: Think of migrating your database like moving physical books to a new library. You don't just dump them in a pile; you must preserve the catalogs (metadata), keep track of the genre sections (namespaces), and ensure the shelving layout (index structure) fits the new building.

cards.title: A Real-World Migration Setup
cards.body: When moving from an self-hosted instance to a managed cloud service, engineers write a script to paginated-read vectors from the source, map the metadata schema to fit the new provider's format, and batch-upload them in chunks.

cards.title: The Re-embedding Myth
cards.body: You don't need to pay OpenAI or your model provider to re-embed all your text from scratch! If you keep the exact same embedding model, you can directly export the raw coordinate arrays and upload them to your new database.

cards.title: Metadata Translation
cards.body: A migration requires mapping not just the vectors, but also the metadata that enables precise filtering.

exercises.prompt: Under what condition is it absolutely mandatory to re-generate all your vector embeddings from scratch during a migration?

options.text: The new database has a different default port number.
You decide to change the underlying embedding model during the transition.
The vector database is hosted on a different cloud provider.
The migration is done in batches rather than all at once.

exercises.explain: Because embedding models output fixed-size dimensions, changing the model changes the vector size and semantic space, making old vectors incompatible with new ones.

exercises.prompt: Is it a best practice to write new data to both the old and new vector databases simultaneously during the migration transition?
exercises.explain: Running a dual-write phase allows you to test the new database's accuracy and performance under live production traffic before shutting down the old system.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: Different vector databases have varying rules for how payload data is filtered, requiring you to carefully map your metadata formats.
exercises.sentence: You must map metadata schemas to ensure your filters still work in the new database.
exercises.explain: Different vector databases represent payload filtering and metadata schemas in unique ways, meaning you must translate these formats during migration.

exercises.prompt: Identify which migration strategy is actually AI-generated nonsense:

options.text: Extract raw vectors using a paginated scan, map the metadata fields, and use batch insertion to load them into the new index.
Convert the vectors into Universal Vector Object Notation (UVON) files, which automatically translates the mathematical dimensions between incompatible models.
Keep the old database running in read-only mode while the new database is populated and validated to prevent downtime.

exercises.explain: Option B shows typical AI-generated hallucination/nonsense, inventing a fake standard called 'Universal Vector Object Notation' which does not exist in database engineering.

exercises.prompt: You are migrating 10 million pre-calculated embeddings from an old database to a new one. Both databases support the exact same embedding model. What is the most efficient and cost-effective strategy?

options.text: Always re-generate all embeddings using a newer model to ensure compatibility.
Discard all metadata and recreate it manually in the new database.
Extract raw vector arrays and metadata, then write them directly to the new database if using the same model.
Convert the vectors into text documents and let the new database automatically vectorise them.

exercises.explain: Re-generating embeddings is the most expensive and time-consuming route. If you keep the original model, you can export the raw vector coordinates directly alongside their ID and metadata, saving massive amounts of API costs and computation time.
